assertions.name: implementer_can_start
assertions.criteria: Read the Technical Approach as an engineer who needs to implement this feature. Could you start coding the CORE FLOW (not edge cases) without needing to ask the spec author any questions about the approach? The spec must answer: what triggers each behavior, where state lives, what new persistence is needed, and what the user-facing flow looks like. If any of these are missing or vague, FAIL.
assertions.description: An implementer could start coding the core flow from this spec

assertions.name: events_tied_to_triggers
assertions.criteria: For EACH distinct behavior in scope (e.g., each notification event, each keyboard shortcut, each visual state), can you identify its specific trigger condition in the Technical Approach? If behaviors are mentioned in Scope but their trigger logic is scattered or missing from Technical Approach, FAIL.
assertions.description: Each behavior's trigger condition is clearly stated

assertions.name: decisions_in_topic_sentences
assertions.criteria: In the Technical Approach section, are the key design decisions stated in the FIRST sentence of each paragraph (or in bold text)? Or are they buried mid-paragraph after reasoning? Scan only the first sentence of each paragraph — if you can reconstruct the major architectural decisions from JUST those sentences, PASS. If you have to read full paragraphs to find decisions, FAIL.
assertions.description: Decisions are in topic sentences, not buried mid-paragraph

assertions.name: no_verbal_tics
assertions.criteria: The spec should NOT repeatedly use formulaic phrases like 'The non-obvious choice here is...', 'The design decision is to...', 'The key decision is...'. One or two is fine. Three or more of these self-referential meta-phrases indicates the spec is DESCRIBING its own decisions instead of just MAKING them. Decisions should be stated as facts, not announced.
assertions.description: Decisions stated as facts, not announced with meta-phrases

assertions.name: no_implementation_leaks
assertions.criteria: Check for implementation details masquerading as design decisions: exact schema syntax, SQL/Prisma/cron expressions, JSON payload shapes, interface definitions, exact new file paths, or mechanism mapping tables. Also check for ABSTRACT implementation leaks: phrases like 'state-transition logic anchored to before/after task values at write time' describe HOW to code something, not WHAT the behavior is. The spec should describe behavior and policy, not algorithms.
assertions.description: No implementation details — including abstract algorithmic descriptions

assertions.name: failure_modes_are_policy
assertions.criteria: Each failure mode entry should state a POLICY DECISION about recovery — something the team could reasonably disagree on. 'Return 404' is not policy. 'Degrade notification links to board-level fallback instead of hiding them' IS policy. Also: are there at least 2 failure modes? If fewer, the spec may be under-thinking edge cases.
assertions.description: Failure modes are policy decisions, at least 2 present

assertions.name: scope_boundaries_clear
assertions.criteria: The Out of Scope section explicitly names things that are ADJACENT to this feature and could easily creep in. Generic non-goals ('don't rewrite the app') don't count. Each non-goal should name something a reasonable implementer MIGHT accidentally include.
assertions.description: Non-goals are specific and prevent realistic scope creep
